justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Both training and serving pipelines designed with clear hot/cold path separation. (2) 100ms P99 constraint thoroughly addressed — latency budget breakdown (40ms typical), 80ms internal SLO recommendation, in-process model to eliminate network hop, LightGBM for <5ms inference. (3) Feature store identified as necessary with online/offline split to prevent training-serving skew. (4) Model versioning via registry with champion/challenger pointer-swap rollback — no redeployment needed. (5) Team size factored throughout: rejected TF Serving/Triton as too complex for 4 people, recommended managed services, phased delivery plan, acknowledged bus factor risk. (6) Trade-offs and risks present in detailed tables covering feature store complexity, weekly retraining staleness, model drift, and feature store latency.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough latency budget breakdown (5ms network + 15ms features + 5ms inference + 5ms serialization + 10ms overhead = 40ms) with 80ms internal SLO buffer — goes well beyond simply mentioning the constraint
Feature store online/offline split with shared feature schema as intentional rigidity point to prevent training-serving skew — identifies the #1 ML production bug class
Champion/challenger pointer-swap rollback is operationally simple and appropriate for team size — avoids over-engineering with blue/green deployments
Pragmatic phased delivery plan starts with hardcoded model and inline features, deferring feature store until needed — respects team capacity
Model-as-artifact pattern (in-process loading) eliminates a network hop and a separate service — correct call for both latency and team size constraints

Recommends gradient-boosted trees (LightGBM/XGBoost) as starting model with explicit reasoning: fast inference, small artifact, works with tabular features — directly addresses model complexity vs latency trade-off
Shadow scoring and canary deployment progression (5% → 25% → 100%) in later phases shows mature A/B testing capability without front-loading complexity
Concerns section honestly flags that weekly retraining may be too infrequent and that the feature store may be premature — shows architectural judgment, not just pattern application

agent_output_excerpt:
# ML-Based Delivery Time Estimation — Architecture Design

## Executive Summary

This is a classic **online serving + offline training** ML system. The architecture separates the hot path (sub-100ms prediction serving) from the cold path (weekly retraining pipeline) using a model registry as the integration seam. Given a 4-person team, I'm recommending a pragmatic design that avoids Kubernetes/Kubeflow complexity in favor of managed services and a simple, auditable model versioning scheme with i
